verbatim_anchor: The failure that worries me the most is the excerpting and getting rightful excerpts
notes: Excerpting correctness > taxonomy placement. Maps to FP-5 + FP-21 (severity class distinction).

verbatim_anchor: one wrong excerpt means the entire structure probably got corrupted
notes: Cascade failure from single error. Core FP-5 principle.

verbatim_anchor: a knowledge corruption is silent and dangerous, and decades worth of study can be built upon it
notes: Silent corruption worst-case. Maps to FP-21 (severity class distinction: silent > visible).

verbatim_anchor: excerpting-bias. Which means: excerpting being based on something else than the source itself
notes: Anti-taxonomy-bias. Maps to FP-4 (taxonomy must not bias excerpting).

verbatim_anchor: the output of the excerpting engine if ran in each of the three scenarios should be exactly the same
notes: Excerpting invariant to taxonomy state. FP-4 core principle.

verbatim_anchor: an overgranulated tree is the most dangerous
notes: Overgranulated > undergranulated. Core FP-9 principle. Cake metaphor preserved in FP-9 explanation.

verbatim_anchor: much like how it's easier to cut-up a cake rather than re-assembling a cut up cake
notes: Cake metaphor for granulation asymmetry. Preserved in B4-SP8 (false contradiction mechanism).
Hafiz check: 01 answer fully captures failure hierarchy, FP-4 invariance, FP-9 overgranulation > undergranulation, FP-5 cascade.
